data.title: Global Security Experts (TSE:4417) Margin Expansion Reinforces Bullish Profitability Narratives
data.description:
Global Security Experts (TSE:4417) just closed FY 2026 with fourth quarter revenue of ¥3.2b and basic EPS of ¥32.42, backed by full year trailing revenue of ¥11.0b and EPS of ¥98.80 across the last four quarters. The company has seen revenue move from ¥8.8b and EPS of ¥67.23 on a trailing basis in FY 2025 to ¥11.0b and ¥98.80 respectively in FY 2026, while quarterly revenue stepped from ¥2.5b and EPS of ¥19.59 in FY 2025 Q4 to ¥3.2b and ¥32.42 in FY 2026 Q4. This frames a year in which higher margins did a lot of the heavy lifting for shareholders.

See our full analysis for Global Security Experts.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this profitability story lines up with the most common narratives investors follow around Global Security Experts.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

13.5% net margin supports higher profitability story

  • Over the trailing 12 months, Global Security Experts converted ¥11,022 million of revenue into ¥1,486 million of net income, which works out to a 13.5% net margin compared with 11.5% a year earlier.
  • What stands out for a bullish view is that this margin profile sits alongside a 47.1% earnings gain over the past year, yet net income stepped up quarter by quarter in FY 2026 from ¥200 million in Q1 to ¥488 million in Q4. This suggests that earnings quality in the data is backed by consistent quarterly profitability rather than a single spike.
    • Supporters can also point to trailing EPS moving from ¥67.23 to ¥98.80 over roughly the same period, which aligns with that 47.1% earnings growth and the higher margin.
    • Anyone worrying that profitability might be fragile has to weigh that concern against the steady climb in quarterly EPS in FY 2026, from ¥13.32 in Q1 to ¥32.42 in Q4.

Curious how numbers like a 13.5% margin and 47.1% earnings growth translate into real world stories investors follow around a stock, have a look at the Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives.

Valuation tension at 27x P/E

  • The shares trade at a P/E of 27x versus the Japan IT industry at 14.3x and peers at 17.1x, while the provided DCF fair value of ¥3,129.32 sits above the current ¥2,671 share price by about 14.6%.
  • Skeptical, more bearish, takes tend to focus on that 27x P/E as rich compared with peers, and the recent price volatility in the last three months. Yet the same data set also shows the share price is below the DCF fair value and backed by trailing earnings of ¥1,486 million, which means any concern about overpaying purely on multiples has to be weighed against a model that prices the cash flows higher than the market currently does.
    • Critics who lean on relative valuation can point to the roughly 10 point gap between the 27x P/E and the 17.1x peer average as a specific reason for caution.
    • On the other side, the gap between ¥2,671 and the ¥3,129.32 DCF fair value shows why some investors might still see room between the current market pricing and the cash flow based estimate.

Revenue growth forecasts versus short history

  • Forecasts in the data show revenue expected to grow around 22.8% per year and earnings about 24.1% per year over the next three years, on top of trailing 12 month revenue of ¥11,022 million and net income of ¥1,486 million, but this sits on less than three years of public financial history.
  • What is interesting for a bullish angle is that these double digit growth forecasts sit beside quarterly FY 2026 revenue that already ranged from ¥2,237 million in Q1 to ¥3,215 million in Q4. Yet the short public history and the highly volatile share price in recent months mean investors who like the growth story still have limited long term data to compare against.
    • Supporters can reasonably highlight that trailing EPS rose from ¥67.23 to ¥98.80 while margins moved from 11.5% to 13.5%, which fits with the growth narrative in the forecasts.
    • More cautious investors may instead focus on the brief listed record and price swings relative to the broader Japan market, using those facts to argue that future growth should be judged carefully against the limited data set.

data.title: Wayfair (W) Losses Keep Margins In Focus As Bulls Lean On 45% Earnings Growth Outlook
data.description:
Wayfair (W) has opened 2026 earnings season with Q1 results that come on the heels of Q4 2025 revenue of US$3.3b and a basic EPS loss of US$0.89, alongside trailing 12 month revenue of US$12.5b and a basic EPS loss of US$2.45. Over the past year, the company has seen quarterly revenue range from US$2.7b to US$3.3b, while basic EPS has moved between a small profit of US$0.12 and losses of up to US$1.02. This sets a clear backdrop of high sales volumes but thin and often negative margins that keep profitability in sharp focus for investors.

See our full analysis for Wayfair.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to set these results against the dominant narratives around growth, profitability, and risk to see which stories the latest quarter supports and which it calls into question.

See what the community is saying about Wayfair

Advertisement

Revenue Stays Above US$3b While Growth Forecast Sits at 5.5%

  • Wayfair generated between US$2.7b and US$3.3b in quarterly revenue over the past year, and revenue is forecast to grow around 5.5% per year, which is below the 11% pace cited for the broader US market.
  • Consensus narrative expects initiatives like CastleGate logistics, Wayfair Verified, and new physical stores to support revenue growth. However, the 5.5% revenue growth forecast and a home furnishings market described as flat to down show that the business is working against a softer demand backdrop rather than strong industry tailwinds.
    • Analysts tying CastleGate and merchandising efforts to higher conversion and revenue per customer are doing so in the context of trailing 12 month revenue of about US$12.5b, which is growing more slowly than the market benchmark.
    • The consensus view that new stores create a sales halo sits alongside forecasts that still only call for mid single digit revenue growth. Investors can therefore see both the upside tools and the current growth ceiling in the numbers.
On top of that, bulls argue those same logistics and merchandising moves could eventually push revenue growth closer to the upper end of analyst expectations, so it is worth seeing how the optimistic case lines up with these figures 🐂 Wayfair Bull Case.

Losses Persist While Earnings Are Forecast to Grow 45.11% per Year

  • Over the last 12 months, Wayfair reported a loss of US$313 million on about US$12.5b of revenue and a trailing basic EPS loss of US$2.45, while forecasts in the data call for earnings growth of about 45.11% per year and an expected move into profitability within three years.
  • Bears point out that losses have grown at roughly 10.6% per year over five years and that revenue growth is forecast to lag the broader US market. This challenges the idea that cost cuts and logistics investments alone will quickly turn those trailing losses into the kind of profit run rate analysts are pencilling in.
    • The move from a recent quarterly loss of around US$116 million in Q4 2025 to projected positive earnings within a three year window would require a meaningful change in the relationship between revenue and costs compared with the recent net income pattern.
    • Concerns about structurally thin margins and high logistics and advertising spend in the bearish narrative tie directly back to the fact that even with quarterly revenues above US$3b, the trailing 12 month figures still show the company in a loss making position.
Skeptics also flag that if those earnings projections slip or prove too optimistic, the cautious case built around the company’s loss history and margin pressures starts to look more compelling 🐻 Wayfair Bear Case.

Mixed Valuation Signals Against Ongoing Balance Sheet Risk

  • Wayfair trades on a P/S of 0.7x, compared with 0.5x for the US Specialty Retail industry and 1.2x for peers, while a DCF fair value of about US$155.93 sits well above the current share price of US$63.93 and the company is reported as having negative shareholders’ equity.
  • What stands out in the bullish narrative is the idea that future earnings growth can justify higher valuation multiples. At the same time, today’s mix of a stock price well below the DCF fair value, a P/S below peer average, and negative equity on the balance sheet gives investors a combination of potential upside and clear capital structure risk to weigh.
    • The data describing the stock as trading roughly 59% below the DCF fair value illustrates why some investors focus on long term cash flow potential, not just recent losses.
    • The flag on negative shareholders’ equity sits directly against that potential upside and may lead more cautious investors to focus first on balance sheet strength before leaning on any valuation gap.

  • The stock has seen sharp recent moves, with returns of 43% over the last 7 days, 56.8% over the last 30 days, and 162.3% year to date. This can change how you think about both risk and potential reward.

Veradermics scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Approach 1: Veradermics Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to what they might be worth in today’s dollars. It is essentially asking what a stream of future cash flows is worth right now.

For Veradermics, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $71.63 million. Analyst and model projections then move through a period of negative free cash flows, such as $69 million in 2026 and $95.50 million in 2027, before reaching a projected free cash flow of $123 million in 2030. Beyond the analyst window, Simply Wall St extrapolates cash flows out to 2035 using its own growth assumptions.

Combining all of these discounted cash flows results in an estimated intrinsic value of about $250.72 per share. Against a current share price around $99, the DCF output implies the stock trades at roughly a 60.5% discount to this estimate, which indicates a wide gap between price and modeled value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Veradermics.

Approach 2: Veradermics Price vs Book

For companies in pharmaceuticals, price to book, or P/B, can be a useful yardstick because it compares what you pay in the market with the accounting value of net assets on the balance sheet. For profitable, established businesses, investors often look for a P/B that reflects both the quality of those assets and the earnings they can support.

Growth expectations and risk also shape what feels like a normal P/B level. Faster growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher uncertainty can point to a lower one. This is why simple comparisons can be tricky.

Veradermics currently trades on a P/B of 30.09x, which sits well above the Pharmaceuticals industry average of 2.63x and the peer group average of 4.33x. Because it pulls these company specific factors together, the Fair Ratio can be a more tailored benchmark than broad industry or peer averages.

In this case, Veradermics’ actual P/B of 30.09x is meaningfully higher than the Fair Ratio. This points to the shares screening as expensive on this measure.

Result: OVERVALUED

Losses easing on a trailing basis

  • On a trailing 12 month view, net loss narrowed from US$200.3 million at Q4 2025 to US$171.1 million at Q1 2026, and basic EPS loss improved from US$1.14 to US$0.96 over the same period.
  • Supporters of the bullish view point to this pattern of shrinking losses, arguing it lines up with the longer term 32.2% annual loss reduction and the idea that Teladoc can move toward better margins even while revenue growth sits at 1.7% a year.
    • That said, quarterly net loss in Q1 2026 at US$63.8 million is wider than Q4 2025 at US$25.1 million, which challenges a straight line bullish story and shows the path has been uneven.
    • The bullish narrative expects profit margins to eventually turn positive, but the trailing numbers still show an unprofitable business, so the data partly supports and partly questions how quickly that margin shift can happen.

Recent loss trends and the mixed quarterly pattern are exactly what bullish investors debate when they argue Teladoc is on the cusp of a more profitable phase, so if that angle interests you it is worth seeing how those arguments are laid out in full 🐂 Teladoc Health Bull Case

Flat revenue trend with modest 1.7% growth

  • Reported revenue over the last six quarters has moved in a fairly tight band between US$613.8 million and US$642.3 million, which lines up with the 1.7% annual growth rate on a trailing 12 month basis and sits well below the cited 11% wider US market growth rate.
  • Bears focus on this modest top line pace, arguing that a roughly flat revenue run rate combined with ongoing losses makes it harder for Teladoc to offset rising compliance, data privacy, and reimbursement costs that are highlighted in the cautious narrative.
    • The Q1 2026 revenue figure of US$613.8 million is lower than Q4 2025 at US$642.3 million, which fits with the bearish concern that growth could stall if competition and reimbursement pressures bite.
    • At the same time, revenue has not collapsed, and the trailing 12 month total of about US$2.5b shows the business still has scale, so the data challenges the idea that the addressable opportunity is already capped.

For readers weighing whether these revenue patterns back up the more cautious arguments about Teladoc's business mix and competitive position, it can help to see how skeptics frame the risks in more detail 🐻 Teladoc Health Bear Case

Low 0.4x P/S against DCF fair value

  • Teladoc trades on a P/S of 0.4x versus peer and industry averages of 2.2x and 2.1x, and the stated DCF fair value of US$11.68 sits well above the current share price of US$6.06, even though analysts expect the company to remain unprofitable over the next three years.
  • Supportive investors highlight this valuation gap, arguing that the combination of a 48.1% discount to DCF fair value and loss reduction over five years makes the current multiple look conservative, while more cautious voices question how long the market will wait for profitability when revenue growth is running at just 1.7% a year.
    • The consensus analyst price target of US$7.10 is only modestly above the current share price, which contrasts with the much larger gap to the DCF fair value and shows that not all valuation frameworks are pointing to the same level of upside.
    • The P/S discount to peers comes alongside continued losses of US$171.1 million on a trailing 12 month basis, so the low multiple is linked directly to the unprofitable status rather than clear evidence of mispricing.
